label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. I need to figure out the order of the fruits from cheapest to most expensive based on the given information. The fruits are kiwis, pears, peaches, loquats, and apples. Let me list out the statements again: 1. Loquats are more expensive than kiwis. So, L > K. 2. Pears are more expensive than peaches. P > Pe. 3. Apples are more expensive than loquats. A > L. 4. Apples are less expensive than peaches. A < Pe. So I need to arrange these five fruits in order from cheapest to most expensive. Let me start by translating the inequalities into a chain. From 1 and 3: A > L > K. So apples are more expensive than loquats, which are more expensive than kiwis. From 4: Pe > A. So peaches are more expensive than apples. From 2: P > Pe. So pears are more expensive than peaches. Putting this all together, we can form a chain: P > Pe > A > L > K. So the order from most expensive to least expensive is Pears > Peaches > Apples > Loquats > Kiwis. Therefore, the order from cheapest to most expensive is Kiwis (cheapest), then Loquats, then Apples, then Peaches, then Pears (most expensive). Let me double-check this. Loquats are more expensive than kiwis (yes, L > K). Pears are more expensive than peaches (yes, P > Pe). Apples are more expensive
